title: Living Hope and Holy Living
subtitle: Elect exiles born again to a living hope, called to holiness in reverent fear

sections.header: Verses 1-2 - "Greeting: Elect Exiles of the Dispersion"

heb.paragraph: The recipients are elect - chosen by God before the foundation of the world. Election is the theological ground of their identity as Christians.
They are exiles, resident aliens in this world. The term denotes temporary residence in a foreign land. Their true citizenship is heavenly.
Originally used of Jews scattered among nations, Peter applies it to Gentile Christians scattered across Asia Minor. They are the new Israel in exile.

refs.note: He chose us in him before the foundation of the world.
You are not of the world, but I chose you out of the world.
They acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth.
Moses sprinkled the blood of the covenant on the people.

notes.note: Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ - his authority is apostolic. The recipients are elect exiles, chosen by God yet strangers in this world.
Election is according to the foreknowledge of God the Father, in the sanctification of the Spirit, for obedience to Jesus Christ. Trinitarian from the start.
Calvin emphasizes that election precedes faith. God chose them before they believed; the Spirit sanctifies those the Father foreknew.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term parepidemos (exiles) emphasizes their transient status in a hostile world. They are resident aliens whose true home is elsewhere.
Sprinkling with blood recalls the covenant inauguration at Sinai (Exodus 24:8) and the Day of Atonement.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that the greeting establishes the theological foundation for the entire letter. The readers' identity as elect exiles determines how they should live.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes emphasizes the social reality: these Christians are marginalized in Asia Minor society. Peter reframes their marginalization as divine election.

hist.historical: Peter writes to Christians in five Roman provinces of Asia Minor (modern Turkey) — Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia. These believers face social ostracism and verbal abuse (4:4, 14) rather than official state persecution. As 'elect exiles' (1:1), they experience the marginalization of those who no longer participate in pagan civic religion, trade guild feasts, and family worship. The letter's household codes (2:13–3:7) address how Christians should live within Roman social structures. Peter writes from 'Babylon' (5:13) — almost certainly a symbolic name for Rome. Silvanus (Silas) serves as secretary (5:12), and Mark is present (5:13). The letter likely dates to the early 60s AD, before Nero's persecution.
hist.context: Peter writes to Christians in five Roman provinces of Asia Minor - Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia. The greeting establishes their identity: elect according to God's foreknowledge, sanctified by the Spirit, destined for obedience and sprinkling with Christ's blood. The Trinitarian formula grounds their election in the work of the entire Godhead.

sections.header: Verses 3-12 - "Born Again to a Living Hope"

heb.gloss: to beget again, cause to be born again
heb.paragraph: God has caused us to be born again. The verb is divine passive - God is the agent of regeneration. New birth is not human achievement but divine gift.
A living hope - not wishful thinking but confident expectation grounded in Christ's resurrection. The hope is living because Christ lives.
The genuineness of your faith - more precious than gold that perishes though tested by fire. Trials prove faith genuine.

refs.note: Unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God.
We were buried with him by baptism into death, so that as Christ was raised, we too might walk in newness of life.
Count it all joy when you meet trials, for the testing of your faith produces steadfastness.
All these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised.

notes.note: New birth comes through resurrection. The inheritance is imperishable, undefiled, unfading - kept in heaven for those guarded by God's power.
In this you rejoice, though now grieved by various trials. The testing of faith is more precious than gold.
The prophets searched and inquired about this salvation. They served not themselves but you. Angels long to look into these things.
Calvin notes that hope is living because it is grounded in resurrection - a dead hope comes from a dead savior.
Faith must be tested. As gold is refined by fire, so faith is purified by trial. The outcome is praise at Christ's revelation.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase living hope uses a perfect participle - the hope is alive and remains alive because Christ rose and remains risen.
The comparative more precious than gold emphasizes value. Gold perishes despite testing; faith yields eternal reward.
Angels long to look into these things - the verb parakupto means to bend down and peer intently.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner emphasizes that vv. 3-12 form one long sentence in Greek - a sustained blessing. Everything flows from God's mercy in regeneration.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes notes the pastoral purpose: suffering readers need assurance that their trials are not meaningless. Peter grounds encouragement in eschatological hope.

hist.context: Peter bursts into doxology: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! The blessing celebrates new birth through resurrection, a living hope, an imperishable inheritance kept in heaven. Present trials test faith's genuineness, producing praise when Christ is revealed. The prophets searched about this salvation; angels long to look into it.

sections.header: Verses 13-21 - "Be Holy, for I Am Holy"

heb.gloss: to gird up, prepare for action
heb.paragraph: Gird up the loins of your mind - a metaphor from tucking robes into a belt for work or travel. Mental preparation for action.
Be holy, for I am holy - a direct quotation from Leviticus. Holiness is not optional but obligatory; it flows from God's character.
Ransomed not with silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ. The ransom metaphor implies slavery and liberation.

refs.note: Be holy, for I am holy - the command Peter quotes.
You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy.
The Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom for many.
In him we have redemption through his blood.

notes.note: Gird up your minds, be sober, set your hope fully on grace. As obedient children, do not conform to former passions. Be holy.
Conduct yourselves with fear - not terror but reverent awe. You were ransomed with precious blood, not corruptible silver or gold.
Calvin sees holiness as both gift and command. God makes holy; we must become what we are. The imperative flows from the indicative.
The ransom price demonstrates value. If God paid Christ's blood, how precious must the redeemed be!

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase gird up the loins of your mind adapts OT imagery (Exodus 12:11; 1 Kings 18:46) to mental discipline.
Futile ways inherited from your fathers likely refers to pagan religious practices. The readers are Gentiles converted from idolatry.
Foreknown before the foundation of the world - Christ's sacrifice was not Plan B but the eternal purpose of God.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes the logical progression: hope (v. 13) leads to holiness (vv. 14-16) leads to fear (v. 17) grounded in redemption (vv. 18-21).

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes emphasizes that the readers' former way of life was not neutral but futile - empty, vain, leading nowhere. Conversion is rescue from meaninglessness.

hist.context: Peter moves from indicative to imperative. The command is holiness - modeled on God's character (Leviticus 11:44-45; 19:2). The motivation: they are to live as strangers in reverent fear, knowing they were ransomed from futile ways not with silver or gold but with Christ's blood, foreknown before creation.

sections.header: Verses 22-25 - "Born Again Through the Living Word"

heb.paragraph: Having purified your souls by obedience to the truth - purification is accomplished, evidenced by genuine brotherly love.
Sincere brotherly love - love without pretense, without mask. Christian love must be authentic.
Born again not of perishable seed but of imperishable - through the living and abiding word of God.

refs.note: A new commandment I give to you, that you love one another.
The grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God will stand forever.
Of his own will he brought us forth by the word of truth.
No one born of God makes a practice of sinning, for God's seed abides in him.

notes.note: Purified souls lead to sincere love, earnest love from a pure heart. New birth is by imperishable seed, the living word of God.
All flesh is like grass - withering, falling. But the word of the Lord remains forever. This word is the gospel preached to you.
Calvin stresses that love is the test of regeneration. Those born of imperishable seed will produce imperishable fruit.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb purified (hegnikotes) is perfect tense - purification is accomplished and its effects continue.
The contrast between perishable and imperishable seed distinguishes human generation from divine regeneration.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that the section rounds off chapter 1 by returning to new birth (cf. v. 3). The instrument is now specified: the word of God.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes emphasizes the community dimension: love is directed toward brothers and sisters. Regeneration creates a new family with new obligations.

hist.context: Peter returns to new birth, now emphasizing its ethical implications. Purification by obedience leads to sincere love. The imperishable seed is the word of God - living and abiding, in contrast to human flesh which withers like grass. Isaiah 40:6-8 is quoted: the grass withers, the flower falls, but the word of the Lord remains forever.

rows.label: vv. 3-12
rows.text: Doxology: living hope and tested faith

rows.label: vv. 13-21
rows.text: Imperative: be holy, you were ransomed

lit.note: Chapter 1 establishes identity (elect exiles), celebrates salvation (living hope), and commands holiness.

themes.note: Holiness dominates as the explicit command. Sovereignty is strong: election, foreknowledge, guarding. Faith is tested and proven.

discourse.thesis: Elect exiles: you are born again to a living hope — be holy, for God is holy.

nodes.text: Peter writes to the elect exiles scattered throughout Asia Minor — chosen according to God's foreknowledge, sanctified by the Spirit for obedience.

Blessed be God! He has caused us to be born again to a living hope through Christ's resurrection — to an inheritance imperishable, kept in heaven. You rejoice though tested by trials, so that your faith may result in praise at Christ's revelation.
The prophets searched and inquired about this salvation. They were serving not themselves but you — things into which angels long to look.

Therefore prepare your minds for action, be sober-minded, set your hope fully on the grace coming at Christ's revelation. As obedient children, do not be conformed to former passions. Be holy, for God is holy.

If you call on the Father who judges impartially, conduct yourselves with fear during your exile. You were ransomed not with silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ — a lamb without blemish.
Having purified your souls for sincere love, love one another earnestly from a pure heart. You have been born again through the living word of God — the word that was preached to you.

coaching.tip: Peter addresses 'elect exiles of the Dispersion' — a loaded phrase. 'Elect' gives identity (chosen by God), 'exiles' gives location (scattered, not home), 'Dispersion' echoes Jewish diaspora now applied to Gentile Christians. The church inherits Israel's vocabulary and predicament: chosen by God, alienated from the world.

The sequence 'a living hope through the resurrection' (v. 3), 'an inheritance imperishable' (v. 4), and 'a salvation ready to be revealed' (v. 5) grounds present suffering in future certainty. Peter doesn't deny the suffering or rush past it; he reframes it. What's coming outweighs what's happening.

'Though you do not now see him, you love him' (v. 8). Peter was an eyewitness; his readers are not. Yet he commends their faith as equal or greater: 'believing in him... you rejoice with joy that is inexpressible.' The beatitude 'blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed' (John 20:29) applies to Peter's audience — and to us.

Prophets who foretold Christ's sufferings were 'searching what person or time' (v. 11) — they wrote without full comprehension. 'It was revealed to them that they were serving not themselves but you' (v. 12). The prophets' words found their true audience centuries later. Scripture is God's communication across time to specific recipients.
